exemplar.title: How does the nature of Patroclus and Achilles' relationship differ between Miller's "The Song of Achilles" and Homer's "The Iliad"?

detailedExemplarData.title: How does the nature of Patroclus and Achilles' relationship differ between Miller's "The Song of Achilles" and Homer's "The Iliad"?

subject.title: English Lang & Lit (Old)
subject.description: IB English Language & Literature combines the study of language and literature, analyzing both literary and non-literary texts across various media and contexts. Students explore how language shapes meaning, identity, and culture through critical examination of diverse text types including advertisements, speeches, novels, and digital media. The course develops analytical skills, cultural understanding, and critical thinking about communication in different contexts and purposes.

strengths: The focus is clearly communicated in the title, introduction, and section headings, showing strong purpose and coherence.
The research question is explicitly stated, narrow in scope, and consistently linked to each analytical section, sustaining focus.
The methodology of comparative close-reading is appropriate and systematically applied to key literary devices.
weaknesses: The introduction is occasionally verbose and could be more concise to maintain precision.
The range of secondary sources is modest and selection criteria are not discussed, limiting methodological depth.
Transitions between some sections (e.g., to Millerâs characterization) are abrupt and could be smoother.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place Topic Communication and RQ in the top band (5â6), though minor lapses in conciseness and occasional digressions justify the lower half of that band (score of 5). Methodology is generally complete but limited in source range and selection justification, fitting the top of the 3â4 band (score of 4). Averaging these performances and weighting the two top-band strands higher yields an overall Focus & Method score of 5.

strengths: Demonstrates solid knowledge of both primary texts with accurate quotations and informed summaries.
Shows awareness of key scholarly debates and integrates primary and secondary evidence effectively in several sections.
Applies literary terminology (symbolism, simile, imagery, kleos) correctly and repeatedly, reinforcing analysis.
weaknesses: Engagement with classical scholarship remains selective, sometimes relying on paraphrased websites rather than peer-reviewed sources.
Key terms (e.g., âhomo-socialâ) are used without definition, which may confuse some readers.
A few phrases misrepresent literary function (e.g., describing Patroclus as âdetrimentalâ); they require revision for accuracy.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Knowledge of topic and sources fits the upper 3â4 band: clear understanding and some effective source use, but not consistently sophisticatedâjustifying a 4. Terminology is mostly accurate and consistent but not flawless, also meriting a 4. The combined performance yields a final Knowledge & Understanding score of 4.

strengths: Uses relevant primary quotations and secondary sources that generally align with the research question.
Delivers sustained comparative analysis (symbolism, narrative voice) with textual evidence and interdisciplinary insight.
Maintains a clear, focused argument contrasting Homerâs ambiguity with Millerâs explicitness throughout the essay.
weaknesses: The pool of secondary sources is narrow, limiting breadth of research and relevance in places.
Some analytical points are descriptive rather than critically evaluative and occasionally lack scholarly backing.
Discussion of limitations and counter-arguments is brief; the conclusion could better acknowledge methodological constraints.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Research quality and relevance, analysis depth, and evaluation each align with the 7â9 band (good rather than excellent). Limitations in source range and occasional unsupported leaps place the work at the lower end of that band. Summing the three strands and weighting them equally supports a final Critical Thinking score of 7.

strengths: Follows the required extended essay structure with logical headings and coherent sequencing that aids navigation.
Meets core formatting conventions: title page with word count, page numbers, and bibliography style.
Sections such as âLiterary Techniquesâ are well signposted, enhancing reader orientation.
weaknesses: Some page numbers in the table of contents do not match actual pagination, requiring consistency checks.
There are sporadic inconsistencies in italics and occasional extra line breaks that interrupt flow.
A few paragraphs and sentences are overly long, affecting readability and requiring subdivision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure appropriateness and layout/formatting both sit in the top band but with minor inconsistencies. The work satisfies all core requirements yet falls short of seamless perfection, warranting a Presentation score of 3.

strandSelections.reasoning: The methodologyâcomparative close-reading supported by secondary criticismâis generally suitable and the essay identifies key literary devices systematically.  A bibliography is supplied and sources are cited, but the range is modest (five secondary items, two of them study guides) and the selection criteria are not discussed.  There is little evidence of wider scholarly research or primary-text edition comparison, so the work meets most but not all features of completeness, warranting the top of the 3-4 band.

Basic formatting conventionsâtitle page with word count, page numbers, hanging-indent bibliographyâare satisfied.  Minor issues (sporadic inconsistent italics, occasional extra line breaks, citation style not fully standardised) prevent top perfection but still reflect correct application overall, justifying 3 within the highest band.

Primary quotations are relevant and generally well integrated; secondary sources, though limited in number, are germane to the research question.  Citations consistently support points made, indicating research that is âmostlyââthough not universallyârelevant.  This aligns with the descriptors for the 7-9 band, at its lower end due to the narrow source pool.

The student announces the focus (âHomoeroticism â¦â) in the title, reiterates it in the introduction and table of contents, and restates it through the essayâs section headings.  The rationale for comparing the two texts is briefly explained and the scope (literary techniques, character arcs, themes) is defined, showing clear purpose.  Minor lapses such as an occasionally wordy introduction and a few imprecise phrases slightly reduce the overall precision, so the strand fits the 5-6 band but merits the lower mark within it.

A clear argumentâMiller explicit vs. Homer ambiguousâruns through all sections and is revisited in the conclusion.  The student evaluates differing narrative strategies and the cultural contexts that shape them, but critical reflection on limitations or counter-arguments is brief.  Consequently, discussion is âgood with partially critical evaluation,â matching the bottom of the 7-9 band.

The essay offers sustained comparative analysis, unpacking symbolism (lyre vs. armour), simile, imagery and narrative voice with textual evidence.  Assertions are generally supported, but some leaps (e.g., âstereotypical role of modern homosexual relationshipsâ) lack scholarly backing and depth.  Thus the work shows âgoodâ rather than âexcellentâ analytical effectiveness, meriting 7 within the 7-9 range.

The piece follows the required EE structure (abstract omitted but no longer required), includes a detailed table of contents, logical headings and coherent sequencing.  Some section lengths are uneven and pagination references in the ToC do not always align perfectly, so a solid but not exemplary score of 3 is appropriate.

The student shows solid knowledge of both primary texts, quoting accurately and summarising episodes, and demonstrates awareness of key scholarly debates (e.g., ambiguity of Homeric intimacy).  Secondary material is used, yet engagement with classical scholarship remains selective and sometimes relies on paraphrased websites.  Overall understanding is clear but not consistently sophisticated, fitting the upper level of the 3-4 band.

Literary terms such as symbolism, simile, imagery, narration and kleos are applied correctly and repeatedly.  Terminology from queer theory or classical philology is largely absent, and a few phrases (âdetrimental to the epicâ) are mis-used.  Consistency is good but not flawless, supporting a score of 4 in the 3-4 band.

The research question is explicitly stated, narrow, and tightly linked to the subsequent discussion of characterisation, technique and theme.  All analytical sections return to the guiding question, demonstrating sustained focus.  Occasional digressions into modern social commentary are present, but they do not undermine the fundamental clarity or appropriateness of the question, justifying a score of 5 in the top band.

annotations.comment: The opening imagery paragraph is very long; divide into smaller paragraphs for better readability.
Some sentences are overly long and complex; break them into shorter sentences to improve readability.
Comparison of motive quotes is insightful, showing excellent integration of primary and secondary sources.
The title precisely communicates the investigative focus and sets a clear purpose for the analysis.
The research question is clearly stated, focused, and effectively connected to subsequent analysis, demonstrating sustained relevance.
The âCategory 2â label is unclear in relation to the essayâs structure; consider removing or clarifying its purpose.
The table of contents provides clear signposting, yet some page numbers in the ToC do not align with actual pagination; verify consistency.
There is no visible page number in the footer here; ensure all pages are consistently numbered.
The introduction delivers solid historical context and relevance, demonstrating good knowledge of the topic.
The opening paragraph is slightly verbose; consider refining phrasing to maintain focus and conciseness.
The âLiterary Techniquesâ section is well signposted and logically placed, aiding reader navigation.
The analysis of Millerâs symbolism is largely descriptive; deepen critical evaluation by linking symbols back to the research question.
The Hammond epithet quote lacks a specific citation; include edition/page details for accuracy.
The five-item list effectively clarifies key features of Greek male friendship, enhancing comprehension.
The term âhomo-socialâ is used without definition; add a brief explanation to ensure clarity for all readers.
Consider explicitly linking how each armour-symbol example addresses the research question in the analysis text.
The simile section header is formatted as plain text rather than a proper heading; apply consistent heading style.
The analysis of the Book 16 simile reveals deep insight into Homerâs technique and is well supported.
The phrase ârole of the bridge remains vagueâ is unclear; rephrase to clarify the analytical point.
The âImageryâ section is clearly marked and enhances structure by signalling a new focus area.
The methodology paragraph outlines planned techniques but lacks discussion of source selection criteria; expand this section.
The comparative close-reading using primary texts is appropriate, but the limited range of secondary sources reduces methodological depth.
The Homeric quotation supports character traits well, but analysis relies heavily on just two passages; integrate more examples.
Describing Patroclusâs role as âdetrimental to the epicâ misrepresents his literary function; revise to âinstrumental significance.â
The transition to Millerâs characterization is abrupt; include a linking sentence to create smoother flow.
A citation is missing for the serving girl reference; include the source to support the claim.
The explicit sexual imagery is incisively analyzed, showcasing excellent application of primary evidence.
The opening sentence on this page feels disconnected; add a brief transition to link it to the previous point.
The analysis ends mid-thought here; ensure the sentence is completed to fully convey the argument.
The narrative style header is correctly applied and frames the analysis of perspective effectively.
The generalizations about modern relationships need scholarly support; cite secondary literature or refine claims.
The Patroclus motivation analysis uses strong primary evidence and adds depth to the characterization discussion.
The term âanti-war stanceâ is asserted without evidence; provide a source or qualify the interpretation.
The bibliography is concise but limited in scholarly range; expand to include more peer-reviewed classical studies.
Citation formats vary across entries; standardize to a single style (MLA/APA/Chicago) for consistency.
The phrase âcharacterizes Patroclus as his individualâ is unclear; reword to accurately convey intended meaning.
The âGrief and Violenceâ header transitions smoothly and helps structure thematic analysis well.
The interdisciplinary insight on burial rites adds strong critical depth and engages broader scholarly debates.
This conclusion paragraph on this page stops abruptly; ensure the argument is fully concluded before moving on.
The conclusion effectively contrasts Homerâs ambiguity with Millerâs explicit intimacy but could acknowledge methodological limitations.
The final summary paragraph synthesizes key findings well, demonstrating clear and focused argument closure.
